questions.question: Which file access mode flag opens a file for output and erases any existing contents?
questions.explanation: ios::out opens a file for output. By default, if the file already exists, its contents are erased. Use ios::app instead to append to an existing file.

questions.question: Which member function moves the read position to a specific byte in a file?
questions.explanation: seekg (seek get) moves the read (get) position in a file. seekp (seek put) moves the write position. tellg and tellp return the current positions.

questions.question: When writing a structure to a binary file, which function is used?
questions.explanation: To write binary data, use the write() member function with reinterpret_cast<char*> to treat the structure's memory as a sequence of bytes, and sizeof to specify the number of bytes.

questions.question: What does the ios::binary flag do when opening a file?
choices: Converts all data to binary numbers
questions.explanation: The ios::binary flag opens the file in binary mode, which prevents the system from translating newline characters (e.g., converting \n to \r\n on Windows).

questions.question: The member function ___ moves the write (put) position to a specified byte in a file.
questions.explanation: seekp (seek put) moves the file's write position. It is commonly used with random-access files to write data at a specific location in the file.

questions.question: To read raw bytes from a binary file into a structure, you use the ___ member function.
questions.explanation: The read() member function reads a specified number of raw bytes from a file into a memory location, typically used with reinterpret_cast<char*> for binary file I/O.
